---
title: "Neural Operators"
subtitle: "Differentiable Physical Modelling and Machine Learning for Sound Synthesis"
author: "Rodrigo Diaz"
date: "2026-06-25"
execute-dir: file
execute:
  cache: false
  warning: false
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: dark
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: true
    smaller: true
    scrollable: true
    code-fold: false
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
    footer: "Week 7 -- Neural Operators"
    transition: fade
    math: mathjax
    auto-play-media: false
---

## Overview

::: {.incremental}
- From PINNs for one setup to operators for many setups
- Functions on grids: encoding, reconstruction, aliasing, and resolution transfer
- Two operator architectures: DeepONet and FNO
- Sound example: a nonlinear 1D string family
- Practical caveats and bridge to deep state-space models
:::

::: {.notes}
The main teaching point is that neural operators are not just large neural
networks on grids. They attempt to learn maps between functions, and every
practical implementation has to decide how continuous functions are represented,
processed, and reconstructed.
:::

# From PINNs To Operators

## Week 6 Recap

PINNs fit one coordinate function:

$$
(\mathbf x,t)
\longmapsto
w_\theta(\mathbf x,t)
$$

. . .

and train it with data, boundary, initial, and physics residual losses:

$$
\mathcal L
=
\lambda_{\mathrm{data}}\mathcal L_{\mathrm{data}}
+
\lambda_{\mathrm{ic}}\mathcal L_{\mathrm{ic}}
+
\lambda_{\mathrm{bc}}\mathcal L_{\mathrm{bc}}
+
\lambda_{\mathrm{phys}}\left\|\mathcal R_\theta\right\|^2.
$$

. . .

The output is a **solution field** for one problem setup.

## The Limitation

Suppose the equation is fixed, but the setup changes:

::: {.incremental}
- a different initial displacement
- a different forcing function
- a different material parameter
- a different damping profile
- a different boundary input
:::

. . .

A standard PINN usually trains a new coordinate function for each new setup.

. . .

That is a solution learner, not yet a reusable simulator.

## The Operator Question

Instead of learning one solution:

$$
(\mathbf x,t)
\mapsto
w(\mathbf x,t)
$$

. . .

can we learn the whole map:

$$
\text{problem setup}
\quad\longmapsto\quad
\text{solution field}?
$$

. . .

This map is an **operator** because both its input and output can be functions.

## Solution Operator

For a PDE written informally as:

$$
\mathcal N[u; a] = 0
$$

where $u$ is the unknown solution and $a$ is the **input problem data**:

::: {.incremental}
- an initial condition, forcing function, coefficient field, boundary data, or parameters
- possibly a scalar, vector, function, or a bundle of several such objects
:::

. . .

The solution operator maps that setup data to the corresponding solution:

$$
\mathcal G:
a
\longmapsto
u.
$$

. . .

Examples:

$$
\mathcal G:
f(x,t)
\mapsto
w(x,t),
\qquad
\mathcal G:
w_0(x)
\mapsto
w(x,t),
\qquad
\mathcal G:
c(x)
\mapsto
u(x).
$$

## Solution Operator

Weeks 1--6 used versions of the same pipeline:

$$
\text{parameters}
+
\text{excitation}
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\text{simulator}
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\text{sound}.
$$

. . .

Neural operators try to learn a surrogate for the middle:

$$
\mathcal G_\theta
\approx
\mathcal G.
$$

. . .

The goal is not one trajectory, but a family of trajectories.

## Why This Matters For Sound

For physical modelling, the expensive part is often not one simulation.

. . .

It is evaluating many related simulations:

::: {.incremental}
- parameter search
- inverse design
- dataset generation
- real-time control over material and geometry
- interactive synthesis with changing excitations
:::

. . .

A neural operator is useful only if it generalises across the family we care about.

# Functions, Grids, And Representations

## The Ideal Object

On paper:

$$
\mathcal G:
\mathcal X
\longrightarrow
\mathcal Y
$$

where $\mathcal X$ and $\mathcal Y$ are function spaces.

. . .

For example:

$$
a \in \mathcal X
\quad\text{is a forcing function,}
\qquad
u \in \mathcal Y
\quad\text{is a displacement field.}
$$

. . .

The operator should not depend on a particular grid.

## The Computer Object

On a digital computer, we store arrays:

$$
a_h
=
\left[
a(x_1), a(x_2), \ldots, a(x_N)
\right]
$$

. . .

and train a finite-dimensional map:

$$
G_h:
\mathbb R^{N_{\mathrm{in}}}
\longrightarrow
\mathbb R^{N_{\mathrm{out}}}.
$$

. . .

This is necessary, but it creates a problem:

$$
\text{Did we learn } \mathcal G
\quad\text{or only}\quad
G_h?
$$

## Same Function, Many Sample Arrays

The same physical field can be sampled on different grids:

::: {.incremental}
- coarse grid: $[a(x_1),\ldots,a(x_N)]$
- fine grid: $[a(x'_1),\ldots,a(x'_{2N})]$
- shifted or staggered grid points
- irregular sensor locations, for architectures that accept coordinates
:::

. . .

For FNO, the input is usually grid samples. Fourier coefficients appear **inside** the spectral layers after the FFT.

. . .

Changing the grid should not change the underlying physical operator.

## Encode-Learn-Reconstruct

The useful pattern is:

$$
a
\xrightarrow{\mathcal E}
a_h
\xrightarrow{G_h}
u_h
\xrightarrow{\mathcal R}
u.
$$

. . .

where:

::: {.incremental}
- $\mathcal E$ encodes or samples the input function
- $G_h$ is the discrete learned map
- $\mathcal R$ reconstructs or evaluates the output function
:::

. . .

The transformation is not cosmetic. It decides what the model can learn stably.

## A Familiar Version: Modal Synthesis

Weeks 2--3 already used this idea physically.

$$
w(x,t)
=
\sum_{\mu=1}^{N}
\phi_\mu(x)q_\mu(t)
$$

. . .

The transform:

$$
w(x,t)
\longleftrightarrow
\mathbf q(t)
$$

makes the linear evolution easier.

. . .

Then the output field or audio signal is reconstructed from the modal state.

## Operator Learning Version

Neural operators use the same broad idea:

$$
\text{function}
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\text{representation}
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\text{learned evolution/map}
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\text{function}.
$$

. . .

Different architectures choose different representations.

. . .

DeepONet uses learned coefficients and learned coordinate basis functions.

FNO uses channels on a grid and learned operations in Fourier space.

## A Larger Literature

Neural operators are now a broad family, not one architecture.

| Family | Representation idea | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Branch/trunk | learned coefficients + coordinate basis | DeepONet |
| Kernel/integral | learned nonlocal integral layers | GNO, MGNO, low-rank NO |
| Spectral | transform to a basis where global mixing is cheap | FNO, AFNO, WNO, LNO |
| Convolutional | continuous-aware CNN-style multiscale maps | CNO |
| Representation-aware | control aliasing between grids/frames | ReNO |

: {.striped}

. . .

The common question is the same:

$$
\text{Which representation makes the operator easiest to learn and reconstruct?}
$$

# Discrete-Continuous Equivalence

## What Should Be Equivalent?

Let:

$$
\mathcal G:
\mathcal X
\to
\mathcal Y
$$

be the continuous operator.

. . .

Let:

$$
G_h:
X_h
\to
Y_h
$$

be the learned discrete map.

. . .

For the same input function $a \in \mathcal X$, two routes should agree:

::: {.incremental}
- **continuous route:** solve first, giving $u = \mathcal G(a)$
- **discrete route:** encode $a$, apply $G_h$ to arrays, then reconstruct a function
:::

. . .

As an operator statement:

$$
\mathcal G
\approx
\mathcal R_h
\circ
G_h
\circ
\mathcal E_h.
$$

## Aliasing Error

Here, **aliasing error** means representation mismatch:

$$
\text{does the answer change because we chose this grid or encoding?}
$$

. . .

For the same setup $a$, compare:

$$
\text{true route:}
\quad
\mathcal G(a)
$$

with:

$$
\text{learned discrete route:}
\quad
\mathcal R_h\!\left(G_h(\mathcal E_h(a))\right).
$$

. . .

The error is just the gap between those two outputs:

$$
\varepsilon_h(a)
=
\mathcal G(a)
-
\mathcal R_h\!\left(G_h(\mathcal E_h(a))\right).
$$

. . .

Small error means the learned array model behaves like the intended continuous operator, not just a grid-specific artifact.

## Why This Is Stronger Than "Works On This Grid"

If a network only learns:

$$
G_h:
\mathbb R^N
\to
\mathbb R^M
$$

it may be accurate at one resolution and fail after resampling.

. . .

Discrete-continuous equivalence asks for a stronger property:

$$
\text{the array computation should correspond to an underlying function operator.}
$$

. . .

This is the difference between learning a table of numbers and learning a physical map.

## Change Of Grid

Suppose we have two grids, $h$ and $h'$.

. . .

If both represent the same continuous operator, changing grids should commute with the learned map:

$$
G_{h'}
\approx
\mathcal E_{h'}
\circ
\mathcal R_h
\circ
G_h
\circ
\mathcal E_h
\circ
\mathcal R_{h'}.
$$

. . .

When this fails, the model is resolution dependent.

. . .

For audio, that can mean different spectra, phase drift, or unstable rollouts after changing sample rate.

## Where Aliasing Enters

The equivalence is only as good as the representation.

::: {.incremental}
- undersampled high frequencies alias
- sharp transients need many modes
- discontinuities are hard for global bases
- boundary conditions can break naive Fourier assumptions
- nonlinearities can generate frequencies not present in the input
:::

. . .

This is why representation choice is a modelling decision, not an implementation detail.

## Why Plain CNNs Are Not Enough

A standard CNN layer is a discrete convolution:

$$
(Kc)[m]
=
\sum_{i=-s}^{s}
k_i\,c[m-i].
$$

. . .

The kernel is tied to a particular grid spacing.

. . .

After changing resolution, the same array kernel no longer represents the same continuous convolution.

. . .

This is the core warning in the CDE/ReNO viewpoint.

# DeepONet

## DeepONet In One Equation

DeepONet represents an operator by combining:

::: {.incremental}
- input samples $\mathbf a = [a(x_1),\ldots,a(x_m)]$, where $a$ is the input function and $x_i$ are sensor points
- a **branch net** $b_\theta$: maps $\mathbf a$ to $p$ coefficients $b_r(\mathbf a)$
- an output query coordinate $y \in \Omega_{\mathrm{out}}$; for a wave problem, often $y=(x,t)$
- a **trunk net** $t_\theta$: maps $y$ to $p$ basis values $t_r(y)$
:::

. . .

The predicted output value at coordinate $y$ is:

$$
\mathcal G_\theta(a)(y)
=
\sum_{r=1}^{p}
b_r(\mathbf a)\,
t_r(y).
$$

. . .

During training we evaluate this at chosen query points $y_j$ -- often a grid, random points, or measured sensor locations.

## The Transformation View

DeepONet makes the encode-reconstruct structure explicit.

. . .

Branch net:

$$
\mathbf a
\longmapsto
\mathbf b(a)
$$

learns coefficients from the input function.

. . .

Trunk net:

$$
y
\longmapsto
\mathbf t(y)
$$

learns coordinate-dependent basis functions.

. . .

At each query coordinate $y$, DeepONet multiplies the branch coefficients by the trunk basis values:

$$
\mathbf b(a)^\top \mathbf t(y)
=
\sum_{r=1}^{p}
b_r(\mathbf a)t_r(y).
$$

. . .

That scalar is the predicted output value $\mathcal G_\theta(a)(y)$.

## Why It Is Operator-Like

The output is not just one vector.

. . .

After training, we can evaluate:

$$
\mathcal G_\theta(a)(y)
$$

at new coordinates $y$.

. . .

That is the back-transformation: latent coefficients become a continuous output field through the trunk.

## DeepONet Caveats

::: {.incremental}
- Sensor locations are part of the representation.
- The branch net may still be tied to a fixed input discretisation.
- Output evaluation is flexible, but input encoding can limit resolution transfer.
- The learned basis must carry the oscillatory or transient structure of the target.
:::

. . .

For sound, high-frequency phase accuracy is a hard test.

# Fourier Neural Operators

## FNO Motivation

Many physical operators are easier in a transformed domain.

. . .

For a translation-invariant convolution:

$$
(Kv)(x)
=
\int
\kappa(x-y)v(y)\,dy
$$

. . .

After a Fourier transform, this global convolution becomes mode-by-mode multiplication:

$$
\widehat{Kv}_k
=
\hat\kappa_k\,\hat v_k.
$$

. . .

where:

::: {.incremental}
- $\hat v_k$ is the Fourier coefficient of the input at frequency $k$
- $\hat\kappa_k$ is the Fourier coefficient of the convolution kernel
- $\widehat{Kv}_k$ is the output coefficient at the same frequency
:::

. . .

So each frequency $k$ is scaled independently. That is the sense in which Fourier space makes the operation "diagonal".

. . .

Multiplication in frequency is the back door to a global spatial operator.

## FNO Pipeline

An FNO first lifts input channels into a feature field:

$$
a(x)
\xrightarrow{P}
v_0(x)
\in
\mathbb R^d.
$$

. . .

Meaning:

::: {.incremental}
- for a string, the input might start as displacement $w(x)$, or displacement plus velocity
- $P$ is a learned pointwise lifting layer
- after lifting, each position carries $d$ channels: $v_0(x) = [v_{0,1}(x),\ldots,v_{0,d}(x)]$
- these hidden channels help predict the output field, but only the final projection has to become physical displacement again
:::

. . .

Each spectral layer then applies:

$$
v_{\ell+1}(x)
=
\sigma\left(
W_\ell v_\ell(x)
+
\mathcal F^{-1}
\left[
R_\ell(k)\,
\mathcal F[v_\ell](k)
\right](x)
\right).
$$

. . .

The learned weights $R_\ell(k)$ act on retained Fourier modes.

## Lift, Spectral Layers, Project

The full architecture is usually:

$$
a(x)
\xrightarrow{P}
v_0(x)
\xrightarrow{\text{spectral layers}}
v_L(x)
\xrightarrow{Q}
u(x).
$$

. . .

where:

::: {.incremental}
- $P$ lifts input channels into a wider feature field
- spectral layers mix global information through Fourier modes
- $Q$ projects feature channels back to the physical output
:::

. . .

This is another encode-learn-reconstruct pipeline.

## What Is Learned In Fourier Space?

Only a finite number of modes are retained:

$$
|k|
\le
k_{\max}.
$$

. . .

The network learns complex-valued matrices:

$$
R_\ell(k)
\in
\mathbb C^{d\times d}
$$

for each retained mode.

. . .

Higher modes are truncated or passed only through the pointwise path.

## Why FNO Can Transfer Resolution

The learned parameters are attached to Fourier modes, not directly to grid indices.

. . .

If a new grid resolves the same modes, the same spectral weights can be applied after an FFT.

. . .

This is the practical reason FNOs can often evaluate on a different resolution from training.

. . .

It is not magic: unresolved content still aliases or disappears.

## What FNO Adds Beyond Modal Methods

Modal synthesis picks a physical basis and known dynamics:

$$
\phi_\mu(x),
\qquad
\ddot q_\mu
+
2\gamma_\mu\dot q_\mu
+
\omega_\mu^2 q_\mu
=
b_\mu f(t).
$$

. . .

FNO keeps a global basis idea, but learns channel mixing at each retained Fourier mode.

. . .

The nonlinear activations between spectral layers then let modes influence one another across layers.

. . .

It can represent families where deriving a clean modal model is inconvenient.

## What FNO Does Not Give For Free

::: {.incremental}
- exact boundary conditions
- exact conservation or passivity
- guaranteed stability under repeated rollout
- correct extrapolation outside the training distribution
- perceptually correct phase at audio rate
:::

. . .

The architecture is a useful prior, not a physical law.

# A Sound Modelling Example

## Nonlinear String Family

The notebook uses a damped nonlinear modal string generator.

. . .

The string parameters and solver are fixed. The setup varies through the initial state:

$$
\mathcal G:
\left(w_0(x),v_0(x)\right)
\longmapsto
\left(w(x,t),\partial_t w(x,t)\right).
$$

## What Goes Into The Network?

Each training example describes **one string setup**.

. . .

In the one-shot notebook:

::: {.incremental}
- initial displacement $w_0(x)$
- initial velocity $v_0(x)$, zero in this generated dataset
- coordinate channel $x$
:::

. . .

Targets:

::: {.incremental}
- future displacement and velocity over a short time window
- or one residual state update for autoregressive rollout
:::

## What Makes It An Operator?

To learn an operator, the dataset must contain many setups:

::: {.incremental}
- different random initial modal displacements
- the same fixed string parameters
- no external forcing in this tiny example
:::

. . .

The same network sees all of these as inputs:

$$
\text{setup}
\longmapsto
\text{solution}.
$$

. . .

With only one setup, it is just fitting one simulation.

## One-Shot Operator

One option:

$$
\mathcal G_\theta:
\left(w_0,v_0\right)
\longmapsto
\mathbf s(t_1),\ldots,\mathbf s(t_T),
\qquad
\mathbf s=(w,\partial_t w).
$$

. . .

This is a direct surrogate for a whole simulation window.

. . .

Good for:

::: {.incremental}
- fast batch prediction
- inverse design
- parameter sweeps
:::

. . .

Hard for long audio because the output field is large.

## Time-Step Operator

Another option:

$$
\mathcal T_\theta:
\mathbf s^n
\longmapsto
\Delta \mathbf s^n,
\qquad
\mathbf s^{n+1}=\mathbf s^n+\Delta \mathbf s^n.
$$

. . .

Then roll out:

$$
\mathbf s^0
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\mathbf s^1
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\cdots
$$

. . .

This looks more like a learned finite-difference update.

## Tradeoff

| | One-shot solution operator | Time-step operator |
|---|---|---|
| Output | whole field/window | next field/state |
| Cost per call | larger | smaller |
| Long-time use | no recurrence error inside window | rollout error accumulates |
| Audio fit | can use window losses | must control stability |
| Physics analogy | Green's function / solver call | FDTD update |

: {.striped}

## Dataset Requirement

Unlike a PINN, an FNO is usually supervised by many solved examples:

$$
\left\{
a_i,\mathcal G(a_i)
\right\}_{i=1}^{N}.
$$

. . .

Those examples often come from a numerical solver.

. . .

So the cost moves:

$$
\text{expensive offline data generation}
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\text{fast online surrogate}.
$$

## Companion Notebooks

Two small FNO examples use the same nonlinear string generator:

::: {.incremental}
- `fno_wave_operator.py`: one-shot map from the first state to the whole short trajectory
- `fno_wave_operator_ar.py`: one-step map rolled out autoregressively
- both compare held-out trajectories and include a simple resolution-transfer probe
:::

# Practical Caveats

## Boundary Conditions

Fourier modes are natural for periodic domains.

. . .

Many instruments are not periodic:

::: {.incremental}
- fixed string ends
- clamped or free plate boundaries
- radiation boundary conditions
- contact constraints
:::

. . .

Common workarounds:

::: {.incremental}
- use padding or domain extension
- use sine/cosine transforms for simple boundaries
:::

## Phase And High Frequencies

Audio is unforgiving.

. . .

Small field errors can become audible when they create:

::: {.incremental}
- phase drift
- wrong modal decay
- missing high-frequency transients
- shifted beating patterns
- unstable feedback in repeated rollout
:::

. . .

A low relative field error is not automatically a good sound.

## Stability

A one-step operator is a dynamical system after rollout:

$$
\mathbf h^{n+1}
=
\mathcal T_\theta(\mathbf h^n,\mathbf h^{n-1},\ldots).
$$

. . .

The training loss may only see short windows.

. . .

For synthesis, we also care about:

::: {.incremental}
- long-term energy behaviour
- bounded output
- response to unseen excitations
- sample-rate changes
:::

## Resolution Transfer Test

A useful test:

::: {.incremental}
- train on grid size $N$
- evaluate on grid size $2N$
- compare against a solver at $2N$
- restrict the $2N$ prediction back to $N$ and compare with the $N$-grid prediction
:::

. . .

This directly probes whether the model learned a function-space operator or a grid map.

## When Neural Operators Make Sense

::: {.incremental}
- many related solves are needed
- the family of systems is known in advance
- offline data generation is affordable
- approximate fast evaluation is valuable
- the representation matches the physics well enough
:::

. . .

They are less attractive when one exact solve is cheap or when the target distribution is too broad.

# Summary

## Week 7 Takeaway

Neural operators learn:

$$
\mathcal G:
\text{function}
\longmapsto
\text{function}.
$$

. . .

Practical implementations use:

$$
a
\xrightarrow{\mathcal E}
a_h
\xrightarrow{G_h}
u_h
\xrightarrow{\mathcal R}
u.
$$

. . .

The representation is part of the model.

## The Main Tradeoff

::: {.incremental}
- Stronger target than standard networks: learn a family of solutions.
- Useful prior: operate on functions, not just fixed vectors.
- FNO advantage: global spectral mixing and practical resolution transfer.
- Cost: many supervised solves are usually needed.
- Caveat: aliasing, boundaries, phase, and rollout stability remain physical problems.
:::

## Bridge Forward

FNOs and DeepONets learn operators between function representations.

. . .

Next question:

$$
\text{Can we learn dynamics in a state space where evolution is simpler?}
$$

. . .

That leads to deep state-space models and Koopman-style views:

$$
\text{nonlinear observations}
\quad\longleftrightarrow\quad
\text{latent dynamics}.
$$

## Resources

- Parker et al. (2022), Physical Modeling Using Recurrent Neural Networks with Fast Convolutional Layers: <https://research.aalto.fi/en/publications/physical-modeling-using-recurrent-neural-networks-with-fast-convo>
- Li et al. (2020), Fourier Neural Operator for Parametric Partial Differential Equations: <https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.08895>
- Kovachki et al. (2021), Neural Operator: Learning Maps Between Function Spaces: <https://arxiv.org/abs/2108.08481>
- Lu et al. (2021), Learning nonlinear operators via DeepONet: <https://doi.org/10.1038/s42256-021-00302-5>
- Bartolucci et al. (2023), Representation Equivalent Neural Operators: <https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/hash/dc35c593e61f6df62db541b976d09dcf-Abstract-Conference.html>
- Li et al. (2020), Multipole Graph Neural Operator: <https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.09535>
- Guibas et al. (2021), Adaptive Fourier Neural Operators: <https://arxiv.org/abs/2111.13587>
- Tripura and Chakraborty (2022), Wavelet Neural Operator: <https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.02191>
- Cao, Goswami, Karniadakis (2023), Laplace Neural Operator: <https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.10528>
- Raonic et al. (2023), Convolutional Neural Operators: <https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.01178>
